Just now

--

Photo by Deng Xiang on UnsplashThe exact same WordPress website can perform very differently depending on how it’s optimized.
In my case, one version of the site took 4.5 seconds to load, while the other loaded in under one second. Both sites used the same content, theme, and images. The difference came down to seven specific changes.
Here is what most people don’t tell you: the seventh change is the one that unlocks the effectiveness of the first six. Without it, the other optimisations can only take you so far.
Let’s go through them one by one.
Why Hosting Matters More Than Most People Think
Before changing any settings, i”s important to understand one thing about WordPress performance.
Think of your website’s speed like a room.
All the common optimizations you’ve heard about caching, image compression, minification, lazy loading stack-up inside that room. But there’s a ceiling, and that ceiling is your hosting provider.
When you improve your hosting, the ceiling gets higher, allowing every other optimization to have a much greater impact.
The first six changes will improve almost any WordPress website. The seventh changes the foundation itself.
1. Measure Before You Optimize
Don’t start changing settings without knowing what needs improvement.
Run your homepage and your slowest page through one of these free tools:

• Google PageSpeed Insights

• GTmetrix
Focus on Google’s Core Web Vitals, specifically:
A. Largest Contentful Paint (LCP):
Measures how long it takes for the largest visible element to appear.
B. Interaction to Next Paint (INP) or Total Blocking Time (TBT):
Measures how quickly the page responds when visitors interact with it.
C. Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS):
Measures how much the page moves around while loading, such as buttons shifting just before someone clicks them.
Take a screenshot of your scores. This becomes your baseline so you can compare improvements later.
2. Optimize Your Images
Images are usually the largest files on a website. For many blogs, they account for nearly half of the total page size.
Here are three simple improvements:
A. Resize Images Before Uploading
Don’t upload a 4,000-pixel-wide image if your content area only displays it at 800 pixels.
Resize images before uploading them to WordPress or any other website platforms.
b. Convert Images to Modern Formats
Use WebP or AVIF instead of traditional JPEG or PNG files.
Plugins like WebP Express can convert your existing images in bulk with just a few clicks or towebp.io is an amazing tools I used everyday with zero ads and it’s really fast without having to have another plugin in the dashboard.
C. Enable Lazy Loading
Lazy loading prevents images below the fold from loading until visitors scroll down the page.
WordPress enables this by default, so avoid disabling it.
Also, make sure your WordPress installation stays updated, as newer versions include important performance improvements.
3. Clean Up Your WordPress Site
Over time, WordPress collects unnecessary data.
This includes:

• Old post revisions

• Auto drafts

• Expired transients

• Unused plugins

• Unused images and files
A. Clean the Database
Plugins like WP-Optimize can remove thousands of unnecessary database entries in a single cleanup.
Database Cleaner is packed with features to handle big databases
Don’t be surprised if it deletes tens of thousands of old rows .
IMPORTANT: Always back up your data before using these or any performance plugin!
B. Remove Unused Plugins
Deactivate anything you’re no longer using. If your site works correctly afterward, delete the database cleaner , duplicate page plugin and other temporary plugins like that, remove them completely.
Simply deactivating a plugin isn’t enough, you should remove it entirely.
Every active plugin has the potential to add scripts, database queries, or additional processing to every page load.
4. Enable Proper Caching
Many posts simply recommend installing a caching plugin without explaining how caching actually works.
Caching has a series of checkpoints.
When someone visits your website, WordPress can either:

• Serve a cached version immediately

• Rebuild the page from scratch using PHP and the database

• The second option is significantly slower.
A good caching plugin stores pre-built pages so visitors receive them instantly instead of forcing WordPress to regenerate every page request.
Popular options include:

• LiteSpeed Cache

• WP Super Cache

• WP Rocket
Enable:

• Page caching

• Browser caching
You’ll usually notice an immediate improvement.
One important point to remember: server-level caching performs much better than plugin-based caching, and I will getto this later.
5. Remove Render-Blocking Resources
When browsers load a page, they often stop to process CSS and JavaScript before displaying any content.
This is known as render blocking.
Most caching plugins include a feature to reduce this delay.
Enable features like:

• Minify CSS and JavaScript

• Defer non-essential JavaScript

• Optimize fonts
For fonts:

• Self-host them where possible.

• Set font-display: swap , so visitors see text immediately instead of waiting for fonts to load.
font-display: swap is a CSS directive that instructs the browser to immediately render text using a fallback system font while the custom web font downloads in the background.
Important
Enable these settings one at a time.
Some themes or plugins may break when aggressive optimization is enabled.
Testing after each change makes troubleshooting much easier.
6. Use a Content Delivery Network (CDN)
Your hosting server exists in one physical location.
If it’s located in the United States and someone visits from Australia, every image, stylesheet, and font must travel across the globe.
A CDN solves this problem by storing copies of your static files on servers around the world.
Instead of downloading files from America, visitors in Australia receive them from a nearby server in Sydney.
Popular options include:

• Cloudflare (Free Plan)

• CDN services included with many hosting providers
If your audience is international, enabling a CDN can produce noticeable speed improvements almost immediately.
7. Upgrade Your Hosting (The Foundation)
Everything we’ve covered so far builds on one foundation:
Your hosting provider.
If you’re using an inexpensive, overcrowded shared hosting plan, every optimization has a limit because the server itself becomes the bottleneck.
For the website, I moved the site to Hostinger.
The improvement came from more than just faster hardware.
Their platform includes:

• LiteSpeed Web Server

• Server-level LSCache

• NVMe storage

• The latest PHP versions

• Custom hPanel for Easy Management

• Automatic 1-click installations

• 99.9% uptime Guarantee

• 24/7 Customer Support
Remember server-level caching from earlier?
This is where it makes a huge difference.
Instead of relying on a plugin to simulate caching, LiteSpeed performs it directly at the server level, making page delivery significantly faster.
Hostinger also focuses on reliability.
Rather than overcrowding servers, they’re designed to stay responsive during traffic spikes.
Another advantage is their free WordPress migration service.
Instead of manually moving your website yourself, their support team handles the migration for you.
If you’re starting a brand-new website, Hostinger also offers an AI website builder that launches sites already optimized for speed from the beginning.
Final Thoughts
WordPress speed optimisation isn’t about finding one magic plugin.
It’s about building improvements in the right order.
Measure first, optimize your images, clean your site, enable caching, remove render-blocking resources, add a CDN, and finally invest in better hosting.
Once your foundation is strong, every other optimization becomes far more effective.
